title: QR Code business cards are the future of conversation

enrichment.summary: Kyle presents his idea of folded cardboard QR code 'business cards' containing up to 127 QR codes per card, each linking to websites, YouTube videos, Google Earth downloads, or legal documents. He envisions carrying many cards to instantly share evidence during conversations, back up arguments with real sources, and fight misinformation. He also discusses plans to sell themed versions on Amazon and briefly pivots to a second idea about wearing personal-achievement data on custom t-shirts.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: novel, underutilized concept that lets anyone instantly share proof during a conversation
QR codes eliminate excuses for not checking facts — refusal to scan is choosing ignorance
we are in an education/attention-span crisis driven by stubborn ignorance and too many media pundits
AOC and Bernie Sanders vlogging to constituents is overdue and a good thing
people should become 'cyborgs' and habitually verify claims by phone every few minutes

stances.topic: selling QR card sets on Amazon
stances.position: viable product — themed sets (immigration law, history, debate topics) are worth building and selling

claims.subject: QR code business cards
claims.claim: The idea is to have little business cards with QR codes that you carry everywhere to show people what you're talking about by scanning the code.

claims.subject: QR code demo
claims.claim: Demonstrating how to use the QR code business cards involves getting your phone into picture mode and scanning the code.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: He thought his video camera was his sofa during the demo.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: He just smoked weed that he grew in his apartment, even though it was premature by about a month.

claims.subject: QR code functionality
claims.claim: The QR code can take you directly to a webpage when scanned.

claims.subject: QR code design
claims.claim: The QR code business cards can have many codes on them, with one side having 127 and the other side being the same.

claims.subject: QR code design
claims.claim: Another version of the QR code card opens up like an accordion and has about 30 codes.

claims.subject: QR code design
claims.claim: The cards can be closed with a rubber band to keep them compact and fit in a wallet.

claims.subject: QR code design
claims.claim: The outside of the cards can be designed fancy and funky since the QR codes are on the inside to protect them from wear.

claims.subject: Photoshop AI
claims.claim: Photoshop's AI can generate images like a rainbow spiral, which could be used for designing the outside of the QR code cards.

claims.subject: QR code applications
claims.claim: The QR codes can be used to direct people to specific information, like Plato's mention of Atlantis, by scanning the code.

claims.subject: QR code applications
claims.claim: The QR codes can link to Google Earth downloads, like a 3D model of the palace of Knossos.

claims.subject: Assassin's Creed
claims.claim: The game allows you to walk around ancient Alexandria and other historical locations realistically.

claims.subject: Google Earth
claims.claim: It can provide 3D models and tours of historical sites, similar to Assassin's Creed.

claims.subject: QR code applications
claims.claim: The QR codes can link to YouTube videos, shorts, TikToks, and Instagram shorts for quick access to content.

claims.subject: Education crisis
claims.claim: There's a crisis where people don't want to look at evidence or do true research, leading to ignorance.

claims.subject: QR code applications
claims.claim: The QR codes can be used to back up arguments with direct links to laws or facts, making it easier to prove points.

claims.subject: U.S. law code
claims.claim: QR codes can link directly to specific laws, making it easy to reference them in arguments.

claims.subject: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
claims.claim: She was seen on her YouTube channel explaining issues related to Elon Musk and Donald Trump, which was the first time Kyle saw a politician vlogging.

claims.subject: Bernie Sanders
claims.claim: He has an awesome YouTube channel where he talks to people.

claims.subject: QR code applications
claims.claim: The QR codes can be used to create cards tailored for specific topics, like immigration law or historical facts.

claims.subject: QR code production
claims.claim: The QR code cards can be made with just a printer, paper, and tape, making them simple to produce.

claims.subject: QR code applications
claims.claim: The QR codes can link to the CIA World Factbook for quick access to country data.

claims.subject: QR code applications
claims.claim: The QR codes can link to historical timelines, like dinosaurs or specific eras.

claims.subject: QR code business
claims.claim: Kyle plans to sell the QR code cards on Amazon, offering them for various topics like immigration law or ancient Rome.

claims.subject: 3D printing
claims.claim: Kyle wants to 3D print the Column of Marcus Aurelius as a Lego-like model.

claims.subject: QR code applications
claims.claim: The QR codes can be used to link to Bible quotes or other religious texts for quick reference.

claims.subject: QR code impact
claims.claim: The QR codes can help society become more evidence-based by making it easy to access proof and sources.

claims.subject: Personal data t-shirts
claims.claim: Kyle has an idea for t-shirts that display personal data like language skills or physical abilities, similar to NASCAR sponsors.

claims.subject: Ancient Rome
claims.claim: In ancient Rome, clothing like togas had stripes indicating status, such as a single stripe for senators or a purple pen for the emperor.

claims.subject: QR code future plans
claims.claim: Kyle plans to create a QR code download page on his website and sell the cards on Amazon.
